After clearing the boards of a
lot of other business at last nights
session, the commission of citizens
end councilmen having in hand the
adoption of recommendations for
the revision of the city charter,
finally reached the point where the
consideration of the Des Moines
plan of municipal government was
next in order.


Councilman b. r. Ostrander,
chairman of the committee which
had previously reported on the
plan to be pursued in the adoption
of the Des Moines idea, called up
the question and asked that the
commission proceed to its imme-
date consideration.


Captain John Gray at this inter-
esting juncture moved for an adj
/ journment. There was no second.


After the consideration and dis
position of another matter, J. M.
Geraghty again called up the Des
Moines plan by asking that the
committee originally appointed on
the question, consisting Of Mr.. Os-
trander, w. d. Vincent and Z. d.
Olmstead, be requested to make a
recommendation pn the practical
bility of adopting the Des Moines
scheme of government without I'M
necessary delay.


Judge Huneke asked II the life
of the commission extended be-
yond the time for the filing of
amendments to the charter for sub
mission at the coming city cleo
ton, and was informed by the
chair that the commissions life ex-
tended until 1t completed its work,
regardless of the approach of the
city election. Judge Huneke then
urged that the consideration of the
Des Mones plan be deferred for
the present. until after other mat
tiers that the commission hoped to
Sup'hit W tiiV iSpDiC az VIC SoftiiiS
election are finally disposed Of.


DR.. Olmstead thought it was
advisable to take immediate steps
to call an election for the choice
Of an official. charter commission
of Is freeholders, with a view of
the Immediate submission to the
people of the Des Moines plan.


Councilman Mohr said he had
heard that the people of Des
Moines was tired of their new plan
of government after trial of 10
months, and were anxious to
change back.


J. M. Geraghty said he would
like to hear the question thorough
1y discussed, as there was merit on
both sides. He thought that with
a nonpartisan city primary and the
abolition of ward lines In the cleo
ton of councilmen, Spokane can
get the chief things that Des
Molnes has in her plan.


DR.. Olmstead said that he had
read the adverse newspaper criti-
cism of the Des Moines plan re-
ferred to by Mr.. Mohr, and be-
lieved 1t was case where one of
the commissioners had proved
recreant to his obligations, and the
people OF Des Moines were about
to invoke the recall against him.
The fact that the commissioners
did not get along well together or
failed to serve the people as they
should, did not necessarily Indicate
that there was failure in the
system. He said that he had re.
Gently talked to number OF men
from Des Moines, and all were well
pleased with their new scheme of
government.


Mr.. Ostrander said that he had
Just received a letter from party
In Des Moines. stating that the
new civic plan was great i'm
provement over the old one.
